research_metadata.research_objective: Evaluate alternative and complementary weather data sources for Scottish Mountain Weather application
research_metadata.geographic_focus: Scottish Munros (Torridon, Glencoe, Coigach, Skye, Knoydart)

platforms_searched: Official meteorological services (Met Office, Norway Met)
Commercial weather APIs (OpenWeatherMap, Windy, Visual Crossing, Tomorrow.io, AccuWeather)

primary_data_sources.citation: [1] Met Office. 'Weather DataHub - Site-Specific API.' UK Met Office, 2025. https://datahub.metoffice.gov.uk/

elevation_capabilities.summit_forecasts: No - Mountain Weather API being retired

limitations: Mountain Weather API not available in new DataHub
No like-for-like replacement for old service

scottish_mountains_suitability.rationale: Official UK weather service with high accuracy but losing mountain-specific forecasts. Would need to use generic site forecasts rather than elevation-specific data.

primary_data_sources.recommendation: Consider as complementary source for general Scottish weather, but not primary mountain forecast source

primary_data_sources.citation: [2] Norwegian Meteorological Institute. 'MET Weather API Documentation.' api.met.no, 2025. https://api.met.no/
key_features: Multiple weather models (ECMWF, MEPS, Arome Arctic)

api_access.rate_limits: Fair use policy - avoid excessive requests

limitations: Optimized for Norway/Scandinavia (may be less accurate for Scotland)
Rate limiting can block excessive use

scottish_mountains_suitability.rationale: Free, reliable API with global coverage. Good for general forecasts but not optimized for Scottish mountains. Worth testing accuracy vs local services.

primary_data_sources.recommendation: Strong candidate for complementary/backup source. Free and easy to integrate.

primary_data_sources.citation: [3] Open-Meteo. 'Free Open-Source Weather API Documentation.' Open-Meteo.com, 2025. https://open-meteo.com/

pricing.pricing_details: Free non-commercial / Paid commercial with API key

data_quality.accuracy: High - combines best models per location

limitations: Temperature jumps at model boundaries possible

scottish_mountains_suitability.rationale: Excellent elevation handling with 90m DEM. Combines multiple weather models including UK Met Office. Free for non-commercial use. Specifically designed to handle mountain terrain with automatic elevation adjustment.

primary_data_sources.recommendation: TOP CHOICE for primary or complementary source. Exceptional elevation handling, free, well-documented, easy integration.

primary_data_sources.citation: [4] Mountain Weather Information Service. 'MWIS Forecasts.' mwis.org.uk, 2025. https://mwis.org.uk/
key_features: Scotland coverage: Northwest Highlands, West Highlands, Cairngorms, Southern Uplands
limitations: NO PUBLIC API - web scraping only
Scraping may violate terms of service

scottish_mountains_suitability.rating: Very High (for data quality) / Low (for API access)
scottish_mountains_suitability.rationale: Gold standard for Scottish mountain forecasts with specialist expertise. However, no API means would require web scraping which may be prohibited and fragile.

primary_data_sources.recommendation: Ideal data source but NO API. Consider contacting MWIS for data partnership or RSS feeds. Use as reference/validation but not automated source.

pricing.free_tier: Yes - Testing tier with shuffled data

limitations: Free tier has SHUFFLED DATA - unusable for production
Rate limits may be restrictive for frequent updates

scottish_mountains_suitability.rationale: Good quality data with multiple models, but expensive for professional use. Free tier unusable for production. Better alternatives available for Scottish mountains.

primary_data_sources.recommendation: Consider only if budget allows. Better free alternatives exist (Open-Meteo, Norway Met).

limitations: Free tier may be restrictive for multiple locations
Costs can add up with high usage

scottish_mountains_suitability.rationale: Good elevation handling and generous free tier. Interpolation approach may be less accurate than model-based forecasts for complex mountain terrain.

primary_data_sources.recommendation: Good complementary source. Generous free tier makes it low-risk to test alongside other sources.

complementary_data_sources.citation: [7] Scottish Avalanche Information Service. 'Live Weather Stations.' sais.gov.uk, 2025. https://www.sais.gov.uk/mountain-weather-stations/
complementary_data_sources.coverage: 6 Scottish mountain areas (Lochaber, Glencoe, Creag Meagaidh, Southern Cairngorms, Northern Cairngorms, Torridon)
complementary_data_sources.integration_method: RSS feed parsing or web scraping
complementary_data_sources.value_proposition: Real-time mountain observations for validation + avalanche risk context
complementary_data_sources.recommendation: Excellent complementary source for real-time validation and avalanche context. Use RSS feeds.

complementary_data_sources.citation: [8] Met Office. 'Weather Observations Website (WOW).' wow.metoffice.gov.uk, 2025. https://wow.metoffice.gov.uk/
complementary_data_sources.value_proposition: Real-time ground truth from actual mountain locations, quality-controlled data
complementary_data_sources.recommendation: Valuable for real-time observations if stations exist near target mountains. Use for validation and nowcasting.

complementary_data_sources.coverage: Scottish airports (Glasgow EGPF, Edinburgh EGPH, Aberdeen EGPD, Inverness EGPE)
complementary_data_sources.value_proposition: High-quality official observations for wind/visibility validation, especially useful for nearby airports
complementary_data_sources.recommendation: Useful complementary source for nearby airports. Good for wind/visibility cross-validation.

complementary_data_sources.citation: [10] Weather Unlocked. 'Ski Resort Mountain Forecast API.' developer.weatherunlocked.com, 2025. https://developer.weatherunlocked.com/skiresort
complementary_data_sources.value_proposition: Specialized mountain forecasts with elevation bands - closest alternative to mountain-forecast.com
complementary_data_sources.recommendation: Strong candidate if pricing is reasonable. Contact for Scottish coverage and pricing details.

complementary_data_sources.citation: [11] Pirate Weather. 'Dark Sky Compatible API.' pirateweather.net, 2025. https://docs.pirateweather.net/
complementary_data_sources.value_proposition: Free minute-by-minute forecasts, easy migration from Dark Sky
complementary_data_sources.recommendation: Good backup option. Very affordable. Test accuracy for Scotland.
Consider if budget allows. Request pricing for Scottish mountain use case.

common_patterns: Most modern APIs use coordinate + elevation parameters for mountain forecasts
90m Digital Elevation Models (DEM) are standard for terrain adjustment
Temperature lapse rate ~0.7°C per 100m is commonly used for elevation correction
Multiple weather model blending is common in advanced APIs
Free tiers typically limit requests/day rather than features
Commercial APIs often hide pricing and require sales contact
best_practices: Always specify elevation explicitly when API supports it
Use multiple data sources for validation and redundancy
Implement reliability calibration when blending multiple sources
Prefer model-based forecasts over interpolation for mountain terrain
Cache forecast data to minimize API calls
Monitor forecast accuracy over time to adjust source weights
Use real-time observations (SAIS, WOW, METAR) to validate forecasts
Implement graceful fallback when primary source fails
pitfalls: Valley-level weather stations may not represent summit conditions
Grid-cell elevation often differs from actual peak elevation
Temperature jumps can occur at model boundaries when blending sources
Free tier 'shuffled data' is unusable (Windy testing tier)
Interpolation-based APIs may lack accuracy in complex terrain
Rate limits can block services unexpectedly
Historical accuracy doesn't guarantee future accuracy for a location
emerging_trends: Shift toward open data (ECMWF now open, Open-Meteo growth)
Retirement of legacy APIs (Met Office DataPoint, Dark Sky)

implementation_recommendations.rationale: Free for non-commercial, excellent elevation handling with 90m DEM, combines multiple national weather services including UK Met Office, well-documented, no API key required, specifically designed for mountain terrain with automatic elevation adjustment.
Completely free, reliable, well-documented, global coverage. Good fallback when primary source fails. Different model sources provide validation.

implementation_recommendations.recommended_solution: SAIS RSS feeds + Met Office WOW API
implementation_recommendations.rationale: SAIS provides real-time mountain observations for 6 Scottish areas. WOW provides citizen weather stations. Both free and provide ground truth for forecast validation.

implementation_recommendations.recommended_solution: Weather Unlocked Ski Resort API (if budget allows)
implementation_recommendations.rationale: Only API found with explicit base/mid/upper elevation forecasts. Updated 4x daily. Designed for mountains. Worth investigating pricing for Scottish coverage.
Free, reliable, high-quality observations from nearby airports. Excellent for wind and visibility cross-validation. Government-run with 99%+ uptime.
1000 free records/day, then $0.0001/record. Includes elevation data. Good commercial-grade alternative if free sources insufficient.
Current scraping works but fragile. No API. Could break anytime. Reduce dependency but maintain as one of multiple sources for comparison.

multi_source_architecture.description: Use multiple sources in priority order with cross-validation

architecture_layers.strategy: Use both sources, compare results, flag significant discrepancies
Real-time observations to validate and calibrate forecasts
Additional elevation-specific data for critical decisions
Maintain current sources as tertiary backup only

conflict_resolution_strategy.temperature: Average multiple sources with elevation correction, flag outliers >3°C
conflict_resolution_strategy.wind: Use maximum wind speed from any source (safety-first approach)
conflict_resolution_strategy.confidence_scoring: Higher confidence when sources agree within tolerances
conflict_resolution_strategy.outlier_handling: Flag and investigate when one source differs >2 std dev from others

data_flow: 1. Fetch forecasts from Open-Meteo + Norway Met every 4 hours
2. Fetch real-time observations from SAIS/WOW every 1 hour
3. Compare forecast sources, calculate agreement metrics
6. Store all source data + blended forecast + confidence scores
7. If primary sources fail, fallback to secondary/tertiary sources
8. Alert if all sources unavailable or disagree significantly

implementation_phases.name: Core Free Sources
tasks: Integrate Open-Meteo API with elevation parameters
Integrate Norway Met API as backup
Test accuracy vs current mountain-forecast.com data

implementation_phases.name: Validation Layer
tasks: Parse SAIS RSS feeds for observations
Integrate WOW API for citizen station data

implementation_phases.name: Commercial Enhancement (Optional)
tasks: Add Visual Crossing as paid fallback
A/B test commercial vs free accuracy

budget_conscious_setup.suitability: Good for small-scale commercial or heavy hobbyist use

professional_setup.suitability: Full commercial application with SLA requirements

recommended_free_approach.risk: Low (free sources unlikely to all fail)

cost_comparison_current_vs_recommended.roi: Infinite - dramatically better reliability at same $0 cost

commercial_sources.redistribution: No (API data for your use only)

mountain-forecast.com.scraping_legal: Unclear - check robots.txt and terms of service
mountain-forecast.com.recommendation: Phase out in favor of API sources
mountain-forecast.com.ethical_consideration: Using paid API alternatives respects their business model

MWIS.recommendation: Contact MWIS for partnership or use RSS feeds
MWIS.ethical_consideration: Public service - likely accepting of appropriate use

general_recommendations: Always include proper attribution in your application
Respect rate limits even when not strictly enforced
Use appropriate User-Agent strings identifying your application
Prefer official APIs over scraping whenever possible
For commercial use, verify license terms or upgrade to commercial tiers
Cache data to minimize requests and respect provider resources
Consider data partnership with MWIS/SAIS for Scottish-specific data

open_meteo_mountain_forecast.description: Fetch Open-Meteo forecast with elevation adjustment for Scottish mountain
open_meteo_mountain_forecast.code:
import requests
from datetime import datetime

# Ben Nevis coordinates and elevation
lat = 56.7969
lon = -5.0036
elevation = 1345  # meters

url = 'https://api.open-meteo.com/v1/forecast'
params = {
    'latitude': lat,
    'longitude': lon,
    'elevation': elevation,  # Explicitly set summit elevation
    'hourly': ['temperature_2m', 'windspeed_10m', 'winddirection_10m', 
               'precipitation', 'cloudcover', 'visibility'],
    'daily': ['temperature_2m_max', 'temperature_2m_min', 
              'precipitation_sum', 'windspeed_10m_max'],
    'timezone': 'Europe/London',
    'forecast_days': 7
}

response = requests.get(url, params=params)
data = response.json()

print(f"Forecast for elevation {data['elevation']}m")
print(f"Hourly temps: {data['hourly']['temperature_2m'][:24]}")
print(f"Daily max temps: {data['daily']['temperature_2m_max']}")

norway_met_forecast.description: Fetch Norway Met forecast for Scottish location
norway_met_forecast.code:
import requests

# Liathach coordinates
lat = 57.6041
lon = -5.4686

url = 'https://api.met.no/weatherapi/locationforecast/2.0/compact'
headers = {
    'User-Agent': 'ScottishMountainWeather/1.0 (contact@example.com)'
}
params = {
    'lat': lat,
    'lon': lon
}

response = requests.get(url, headers=headers, params=params)
data = response.json()

# Extract next 24 hours
for timeseries in data['properties']['timeseries'][:24]:
    time = timeseries['time']
    instant = timeseries['data']['instant']['details']
    print(f"{time}: {instant['air_temperature']}°C, "
          f"Wind: {instant['wind_speed']} m/s {instant.get('wind_from_direction', 'N/A')}°")

sais_rss_parser.description: Parse SAIS RSS feed for Torridon area
sais_rss_parser.code:
import feedparser
from datetime import datetime

# SAIS Torridon RSS feed
feed_url = 'https://torridonblog.sais.gov.uk/feed'

feed = feedparser.parse(feed_url)

print(f"SAIS Torridon - Last updated: {feed.feed.get('updated', 'N/A')}")
print(f"\nLatest report:")

if feed.entries:
    latest = feed.entries[0]
    print(f"Title: {latest.title}")
    print(f"Published: {latest.published}")
    print(f"Summary: {latest.summary[:200]}...")
    print(f"Link: {latest.link}")
    
    # Could parse summary for weather observations
    # Temperature, wind, conditions etc.

metar_fetch.description: Fetch METAR for nearby Scottish airport
metar_fetch.code:
import requests

# Inverness Airport (nearest to many Highland mountains)
icao = 'EGPE'
url = 'https://aviationweather.gov/api/data/metar'
params = {
    'ids': icao,
    'format': 'json'
}

response = requests.get(url, params=params)
data = response.json()

for metar in data:
    print(f"Station: {metar['icaoId']}")
    print(f"Time: {metar['obsTime']}")
    print(f"Raw: {metar['rawOb']}")
    print(f"Temp: {metar.get('temp')}°C")
    print(f"Wind: {metar.get('wspd')} kt from {metar.get('wdir')}°")
    print(f"Visibility: {metar.get('visib')} SM")
    print(f"Clouds: {metar.get('cover')}")

multi_source_aggregator.description: Aggregate forecasts from multiple sources with conflict detection
multi_source_aggregator.code:
import requests
from statistics import mean, stdev
from typing import List, Dict

class MultiSourceWeatherAggregator:
    def __init__(self, lat: float, lon: float, elevation: int):
        self.lat = lat
        self.lon = lon
        self.elevation = elevation
        
    def fetch_open_meteo(self) -> Dict:
        """Fetch from Open-Meteo"""
        url = 'https://api.open-meteo.com/v1/forecast'
        params = {
            'latitude': self.lat,
            'longitude': self.lon,
            'elevation': self.elevation,
            'hourly': 'temperature_2m,windspeed_10m,precipitation',
            'timezone': 'Europe/London'
        }
        return requests.get(url, params=params).json()
    
    def fetch_norway_met(self) -> Dict:
        """Fetch from Norway Met"""
        url = 'https://api.met.no/weatherapi/locationforecast/2.0/compact'
        headers = {'User-Agent': 'MountainWeather/1.0 (contact@example.com)'}
        params = {'lat': self.lat, 'lon': self.lon}
        return requests.get(url, headers=headers, params=params).json()
    
    def normalize_sources(self, open_meteo_data: Dict, norway_met_data: Dict) -> Dict:
        """Normalize different API formats to common structure"""
        # Implementation would parse each source format
        # and convert to common schema
        pass
    
    def detect_conflicts(self, normalized_data: List[Dict]) -> List[str]:
        """Detect significant discrepancies between sources"""
        conflicts = []
        
        # Extract temperatures from all sources
        temps = [d['temperature'] for d in normalized_data]
        
        if len(temps) > 1:
            avg_temp = mean(temps)
            std_temp = stdev(temps)
            
            for i, temp in enumerate(temps):
                if abs(temp - avg_temp) > 3:  # More than 3°C difference
                    conflicts.append(
                        f"Source {i}: Temperature {temp}°C differs from "
                        f"average {avg_temp:.1f}°C by {abs(temp - avg_temp):.1f}°C"
                    )
        
        return conflicts
    
    def blend_forecasts(self, normalized_data: List[Dict]) -> Dict:
        """Create reliability-calibrated blended forecast"""
        blended = {}
        
        # Temperature: average with outlier detection
        temps = [d['temperature'] for d in normalized_data]
        blended['temperature'] = mean(temps)
        blended['temperature_confidence'] = 1.0 - (stdev(temps) / 10)
        
        # Wind: use maximum (safety-first)
        winds = [d['wind_speed'] for d in normalized_data]
        blended['wind_speed'] = max(winds)
        blended['wind_confidence'] = 1.0 if max(winds) == min(winds) else 0.7
        
        # Precipitation: use maximum (conservative)
        precips = [d['precipitation'] for d in normalized_data]
        blended['precipitation'] = max(precips)
        
        return blended

# Usage
aggregator = MultiSourceWeatherAggregator(
    lat=57.6041,  # Liathach
    lon=-5.4686,
    elevation=1053
)

open_meteo = aggregator.fetch_open_meteo()
norway_met = aggregator.fetch_norway_met()

normalized = aggregator.normalize_sources(open_meteo, norway_met)
conflicts = aggregator.detect_conflicts(normalized)
blended = aggregator.blend_forecasts(normalized)

if conflicts:
    print("WARNING: Forecast conflicts detected:")
    for conflict in conflicts:
        print(f"  - {conflict}")

print(f"\nBlended forecast:")
print(f"Temperature: {blended['temperature']:.1f}°C "
      f"(confidence: {blended['temperature_confidence']:.0%})")
print(f"Wind: {blended['wind_speed']:.1f} km/h "
      f"(confidence: {blended['wind_confidence']:.0%})")

immediate_actions: Implement Open-Meteo as primary source with elevation parameters
Add Norway Met as automatic failover backup
Parse SAIS RSS feeds for real-time observations
Test accuracy of new sources vs current mountain-forecast.com for 2-4 weeks
short_term_1_3_months: Integrate WOW API for citizen weather station data
Implement multi-source validation and conflict detection
Build confidence scoring system for blended forecasts
medium_term_3_6_months: Evaluate Weather Unlocked if budget allows
Collect accuracy statistics to optimize source weights
Implement reliability calibration for forecast blending
Consider reaching out to MWIS for official data partnership
long_term_6_12_months: Analyze forecast accuracy patterns by location and season
Build machine learning model to weight sources dynamically
Consider premium sources if accuracy improvements justify cost
Contribute findings back to open-source weather community
critical_success_factors: Free primary sources (Open-Meteo + Norway Met) eliminate cost barrier
Multiple redundant sources dramatically improve reliability
Elevation-specific adjustments critical for Scottish mountains
Community and official sources provide best of both worlds
